profile.executiveSummary: AI Strategist & Anthropologist | Pilot | Futurist | Outspoken Mental Health Advocate   Cliff Jurkiewicz is a futurist who has accurately predicted technological advancements throughout a 30-year operational career spanning aviation, AI, and enterprise transformation. As Vice President of Global Strategy and Executive Evangelist at Phenom, he translates the chaos of Applied AI into clear business direction, defining the company’s leadership in talent intelligence.  Cliff focuses on the structural mechanics of the future of work. He is the architect of foundational industry models—including the Work Blueprint, Platform as a Practice, the AI Fluency Scorecard, and the Illusion of Choice—designed to help global organizations shift from rigid job structures to dynamic, skills-based ecosystems.  A featured voice at CERN, MIT, Nasdaq, and NASA, Cliff is a provocateur who forces the world's most influential executives to confront uncomfortable truths. He challenges leaders to dismantle weak assumptions and operationalize accountability. His approach simplifies complexity and mitigates risk, ensuring that as technology accelerates, human capability remains the competitive advantage.  A pilot, musician, former restaurateur, and outspoken mental health advocate, Cliff brings curiosity and empathy to every conversation. His personal mission is shaped by his family. He honors his late son through Kyle’s Wish, a nonprofit that inspires recovery through aviation experiences for young people facing mental health challenges. He finds purpose in raising his sons Phoenix and Sagan with his wife Gina. These experiences reinforce his belief that the future of work must serve people first.  #Futurism #AI #FutureOfWork #MentalHealth #AppliedAI
profile.summary: Accomplished restaurant and hospitality executive with 20+ years building a beloved regional fast-food chain from a single location to 14 stores across the Southwest. Known for operational precision, supply chain mastery, community philanthropy, and an uncompromising commitment to quality standards.

workHistory.title: Co-Founder/Chief Pilot
workHistory.description: Aviation Experiences For People In Recovery – Inspiring recovery through the experience of flight.  Kyle's Wish Foundation is a registered 501(c)(3) charity owned and operated by Cliff and Gina Jurkiewicz, who's mission is providing free flights for treatment to people seeking mental health treatment, recovery, family visitation and self-discovery.   Kyle's Wish is inspired by Cliff's late son, Kyle who, above all things, cherished his time with friends and family. Kyle passed away at the age of 26 in June of 2020 after a long battle with mental illness and drug use.

About the Company

Popular beverage alcohol e-commerce platform

Industry
Internet

Type
Privately Held, VC-backed

About the Role

The Company is seeking a Vice President of Enterprise Sales to spearhead the expansion of its beverage offerings within the restaurant industry. The successful candidate will be tasked with developing and executing a robust sales strategy to secure partnerships with major restaurant chains and foodservice providers. This pivotal role demands a seasoned professional with a background in restaurant operations or marketing, capable of leveraging industry expertise to build and maintain strong relationships, drive sales, and meet revenue targets. Key responsibilities include market expansion, team leadership, collaboration with cross-functional teams, and a focus on customer success and retention.
Applicants for the Vice President of Enterprise Sales position at the company should hold a Bachelor’s degree in business administration, marketing, or a related field, with an MBA preferred. A minimum of 3 years' experience in leadership roles within the restaurant industry is required, with a proven track record of success. The ideal candidate will have a strong understanding of the industry's dynamics, excellent leadership and communication skills, and the ability to develop and execute successful sales strategies. Experience in contract negotiation, relationship management, and proficiency in CRM software and Microsoft Office Suite are also essential. The role demands a strategic thinker with strong analytical skills, the ability to use data for decision-making, and a willingness to travel as needed.

Travel Percent
Less than 10%

• Sales/Revenue

Vice President of Development
Position Summary

The Vice President of Development is the organization's lead fundraising strategist and relationship-builder, responsible for designing, leading, and executing a comprehensive development program that strengthens community support and advances the mission and long-term sustainability of the organization. This role oversees and manages a six-person Development team, providing strategic direction, mentorship, and accountability across all fundraising functions.

Reporting directly to the CEO and serving on the Leadership Team, the VP oversees all philanthropic revenue streams-including major gifts, annual giving, institutional support, planned giving, and events-and plays a key leadership role in planning and executing an upcoming major fundraising campaign.

The Vice President partners closely with the CEO, Chief of Staff, Board of Directors, Development Committee, and cross-functional colleagues, including the Director of Public Relations & Marketing, to align fundraising strategy, communications, and engagement efforts.

Key Responsibilities

Organizational & Strategic Leadership
• Serve as a member of the Leadership Team, contributing to organizational strategy, planning, and decision-making.
• Partner closely with the CEO to advance fundraising priorities, donor relationships, and campaign leadership.
• Act as a visible and credible ambassador with donors, institutional partners, and community stakeholders.
• Collaborate with and support the Board of Directors and Development Committee in fulfilling their fundraising and ambassadorial roles.

Fundraising Strategy & Execution
• Design, implement, and refine a comprehensive fundraising strategy aligned with organizational priorities and growth goals.
• Provide leadership for all development functions, including:
• Major and principal gifts
• Annual fund and mid-level giving
• Foundation and corporate philanthropy
• Planned and legacy giving
• Special events and campaigns
• Development operations
• Set annual and multi-year revenue goals; monitor performance, analyze results, and adapt strategies as needed.
• Stay current on emerging trends and best practices in philanthropy, donor engagement, and campaign fundraising.

Major Gifts & Campaign Leadership
• In concert with the CEO and Chief of Staff, lead strategy and execution for a major fundraising campaign, including planning, donor strategy, solicitation sequencing, and stewardship.
• Personally manage and solicit a portfolio of high-capacity donors and prospects.
• Partner with the CEO, Board members, and senior leaders on donor cultivation and solicitation.
• Advance a culture of relationship-based fundraising focused on long-term partnerships and multi-year commitments.

Team Leadership & Management
• Lead, mentor, and support a high-performing Development team; set clear goals, expectations, and accountability measures.
• Foster a collaborative, inclusive, and growth-oriented team culture.

Client Partner
Role Description

Who we are:

Born digital, UST transforms lives through the power of technology. We walk alongside our clients and partners, embedding innovation and agility into everything they do. We help them create transformative experiences and human-centered solutions for a better world.

UST is a mission-driven group of 29,000+ practical problem solvers and creative thinkers in more than 30 countries. Our entrepreneurial teams are empowered to innovate, act nimbly, and create a lasting and sustainable impact for our clients, their customers, and the communities in which we live.

With us, you’ll create a boundless impact that transforms your career—and the lives of people across the world.

Visit us at .

You Are

A Client Partner that will assume leadership several key customers. In this role you will balance business development and sales responsibilities including identifying, qualifying, and closing new business opportunities. savedJobs.rawText:
Vice President, Client Growth - Enterprise Partnership Group
Job Description

The Vice President, Client Growth is a dynamic and growth-oriented executive responsible for acquiring new business, expanding client relationships within vertical markets, and ensuring long-term growth and retention. Reporting to the Chief Growth Officer for the Enterprise Partnership Group at Aramark, this strategic leader will identify and cultivate new opportunities across lines of business, lead client acquisition efforts, and collaborate with internal teams to design tailored solutions aligned with Aramark’s strategic objectives. Success in this role requires a combination of strategic thinking, strong sales acumen, and deep market insight. This leader will bring a proven track record of generating new business, closing high-value deals, and building long-term relationships with key decision-makers at target organizations.

The ideal candidate will bring a proven record of securing enterprise-level accounts across complex and highly competitive markets. They will demonstrate success in selling managed services at scale while building strong partnerships and long-term relationships across the enterprise. Their career history should reflect measurable achievements, recognition through awards or performance metrics, and a pattern of progressive professional growth.

Job Responsibilities

• New Business Acquisition: Lead prospecting, qualification, and development of new business across verticals. Build and manage a robust pipeline to drive sustained growth.
• Executive Relationship Development: Cultivate senior-level relationships with client decision-makers, positioning Aramark as a strategic partner. Drive executive engagement and influence across client organizations.
• Strategic Account & Vertical Growth: Oversee account planning and lead vertical expansion efforts. Guide cross-functional teams in retention and growth strategies.
• Sales Strategy & Execution: Develop and execute sales strategies to meet revenue targets. Lead the full sales cycle—from lead generation through proposal, negotiation, and contract execution.
• Client Lifecycle Management: Ensure long-term client value and satisfaction from acquisition through retention.
• Cross-Functional Collaboration: Partner with operations, marketing, legal, and finance to deliver seamless client solutions and mobilization.
• Proposal & Negotiation Leadership: Lead development of proposals, RFPs, and presentations. Drive strategic negotiations for high-value deals and vertical growth.
• Pipeline & Forecasting: Maintain accurate sales forecasting and reporting. Use CRM tools (e.g., Salesforce) to track pipeline health and provide leadership updates.
• Financial Strategy Alignment: Champion financial planning that aligns client outcomes with internal execution and commercial goals.
• Client Feedback & Continuous Improvement: Leverage client insights to refine offerings and improve sales strategies. Continuously evolve approach based on market feedback.

Qualifications

Aramar

savedJobs.rawText:
VP, Client Partnerships – Conference Marketing
EMC Outdoor helps brands connect with audiences through creative, measurable out-of-home advertising campaigns. We specialize in location-based marketing and experiential activations and have led the industry in the development of OOH media for conference and trade show activations for over 30 years.

Vice President, Client Partnerships – Conference Marketing

We're seeking an energetic, results-driven VP of Client Partnerships to drive revenue growth and business development in our Conference Marketing division.

In this role, you'll own new business acquisition, nurture and expand existing client relationships, and act as a trusted advisor in the conference marketing space. You'll build relationships with exhibitors, agencies, key associations, and event organizers — and help position us as the go-to partner for OOH media at conferences and tradeshows.

What you'll do:
• Drive new business development and manage a lead generation pipeline to hit annual revenue targets
• Grow and retain existing client relationships through a consultative, strategic approach
• Collaborate cross-functionally with marketing, and media operations to support company growth
• Oversee proposals, contracts, and new offerings to ensure best-in-class client deliverables

What you bring:
• Proven track record in business development and client relationship management
• Deep understanding of the conference, tradeshow, or events ecosystem
• Strong leadership skills with experience managing a team
• Excellent communication, negotiation, and strategic thinking skills
• Comfort with CRM management, reporting, and hitting KPIs
• Experience in the pharmaceutical and healthcare category is not required, but highly valuable

If you thrive in a fast-paced, client-facing environment and are passionate about the conference marketing space, we'd love to hear from you.

Senior Vice President, Philanthropic Consulting
The Search

National Philanthropic Trust (NPT) seeks a strategic, forward-thinking, and collaborative leader to serve as its first Senior Vice President of Philanthropic Consulting.

This is a unique opportunity to join a high-impact philanthropic organization at a pivotal moment of transformation, and to lead and expand its philanthropy consulting practice. The Senior Vice President will oversee a current team of seven in the growing practice, help to steward NPT’s larger programmatic vision, and serve as a trusted advisor to the Chief Philanthropy Officer and the operating committee of the organization.

The Senior Vice President will be an experienced leader who is passionate about shaping strategy, fostering talent, and deepening impact. They will bring a drive for service and outcomes, have an appetite for business development, and possess a keen understanding of the philanthropic ecosystem. The successful candidate will have a proven track record of developing a client base and leading teams in delivering world-class strategies, be energized by the opportunity to build institutional capacity and further elevate a high-performing team and be excited to help chart the next chapter of NPT’s work supporting philanthropy globally.

National Philanthropic Trust has retained the search firm Isaacson, Miller to assist with this critical recruitment. Instructions for applying, nominating, and inquiring about the position are located at the end of this document.

Our Story

National Philanthropic Trust is the leading partner for donor-advised giving, providing donors, family offices, foundations, and financial service institutions with the solutions and expertise needed to transform philanthropic intention into action.

Since its founding in 1996, NPT has raised more than $80.2 billion in charitable contributions and currently manages more than $65 billion in charitable assets. As the largest independent sponsor of donor-advised funds (DAFs), NPT ranks among the largest grant-making institutions in the United States. It has made more than 930,000 grants, totaling over $37.7 billion, to nonprofit organizations worldwide.

National Philanthropic Trust has a proud history of innovation. In 2000, it became the first independent organization to offer a private-label DAF program for financial and wealth management institutions. In 2013, NPT’s first international affiliate, NPT UK, was established to serve the needs of philanthropists who choose to base their giving in the U.K. In 2021, the organization launched Giving Point, a full-service online platform for donors to manage their NPT DAFs.

Under the new leadership of CEO Holly Welch Stubbing, a nationally recognized leader in philanthropy with over 25 years of experience and thought leadership on giving and grantmaking, the organization is poised for transformation as it charts a bold course for growth and impact. Vice President of Development
Position Summary

The Vice President of Development is the organization's lead fundraising strategist and relationship-builder, responsible for designing, leading, and executing a comprehensive development program that strengthens community support and advances the mission and long-term sustainability of the organization. This role oversees and manages a six-person Development team, providing strategic direction, mentorship, and accountability across all fundraising functions.

Reporting directly to the CEO and serving on the Leadership Team, the VP oversees all philanthropic revenue streams-including major gifts, annual giving, institutional support, planned giving, and events-and plays a key leadership role in planning and executing an upcoming major fundraising campaign.

The Vice President partners closely with the CEO, Chief of Staff, Board of Directors, Development Committee, and cross-functional colleagues, including the Director of Public Relations & Marketing, to align fundraising strategy, communications, and engagement efforts.
Key ResponsibilitiesOrganizational & Strategic Leadership
• Serve as a member of the Leadership Team, contributing to organizational strategy, planning, and decision-making.
• Partner closely with the CEO to advance fundraising priorities, donor relationships, and campaign leadership.
• Act as a visible and credible ambassador with donors, institutional partners, and community stakeholders.
• Collaborate with and support the Board of Directors and Development Committee in fulfilling their fundraising and ambassadorial roles.

Fundraising Strategy & Execution
• Design, implement, and refine a comprehensive fundraising strategy aligned with organizational priorities and growth goals.
• Provide leadership for all development functions, including:
• Major and principal gifts
• Annual fund and mid-level giving
• Foundation and corporate philanthropy
• Planned and legacy giving
• Special events and campaigns
• Development operations
• Set annual and multi-year revenue goals; monitor performance, analyze results, and adapt strategies as needed.
• Stay current on emerging trends and best practices in philanthropy, donor engagement, and campaign fundraising.

Major Gifts & Campaign Leadership
• In concert with the CEO and Chief of Staff, lead strategy and execution for a major fundraising campaign, including planning, donor strategy, solicitation sequencing, and stewardship.
• Personally manage and solicit a portfolio of high-capacity donors and prospects.
• Partner with the CEO, Board members, and senior leaders on donor cultivation and solicitation.
• Advance a culture of relationship-based fundraising focused on long-term partnerships and multi-year commitments.

Team Leadership & Management
• Lead, mentor, and support a high-performing Development team; set clear goals, expectations, and accountability measures.
• Foster a collaborative, inclusive, and growth-oriented team culture.
• Ensure effective systems, process

About the Role

The Company is seeking a Vice President of Product Management to play a pivotal role in the organization's mission to provide high-quality, knowledge-rich curricula that meet the needs of educators and drive student outcomes. The successful candidate will be responsible for owning the product roadmap, ensuring its alignment with the company's vision, and leading the execution of clear, actionable plans across the product portfolio. This executive will be a key leader in the product organization, working closely with the Chief Product Officer to translate strategy into practice and to maintain a strong focus on market demands and internal development capacity. The role requires a seasoned professional with a proven track record in product management, particularly in the education or mission-driven sectors, and a strong background in curriculum, edtech, or related industries.

Key responsibilities for the Vice President of Product Management include leading the team in the development and maintenance of product roadmaps, managing the P&L across product lines, and ensuring that the product development process is in line with the company's goals. The ideal candidate will have at least 15 years of product management experience, with a minimum of 10 years in senior leadership roles, and a Bachelor's degree (Master's preferred). They must be adept at fostering a culture of customer-centric innovation, collaboration, and accountability, and be able to influence at all levels of the organization. Operational excellence, team leadership, and cross-functional collaboration are also critical aspects of the role, as is the ability to balance long-term vision with short-term execution and to model mission-driven leadership in pursuit of the company's objectives.

Efficiency is finite. Revenue is infinite.

We are staring at a moment in history where we can finally stop managing "corporate sludge" and start managing talent.

We are moving past the concept of "Human-in-the-Loop," which is just a nice way of saying humans are there to check the AI's homework. The future is "Human-in-the-Lead," where humans drive the outcomes while AI handles the complexity.

I will be breaking down the "Work Blueprint," explaining why traditional job descriptions are obsolete, and showing how we shift our focus from simple ROI to Revenue Engineering.

posts.content:
There is a saying I live by: You keep what you have by giving it away. 

As 2025 closes, I am looking at the data.

This map represents 19,123 nautical miles and 170 flight hours.

That is a significant amount of time in the air, but the truth is, I flew less in 2025 than I did in 2024.

People often talk about seeking "balance" in life. I don't believe such a thing exists. You cannot balance everything equally; you have to prioritize where you spend your time based on what the machine needs.

This year, we faced specific family and work challenges. Those took priority. The plane stayed in the hangar a bit more, so I could be present on the ground.

It's funny though, when people see a private plane, they assume "wealth." I want to correct the record. I am not rich. 

My wife Gina and I are certainly blessed. But we don't own vacation houses and our home is quite modest. We don't collect material things or take expensive vacations. We drive a couple of older Fords. We work hard every day and will have to for a long time to come. 

We have simply made a choice. The disposable income that could go toward luxuries goes into our mission instead. It goes into Avgas and maintenance. It goes into the $267 per hour it costs to keep this aircraft operational not including what the plane itself costs. 

Even with the reduced hours, the priorities remained clear:
 
15% Work: Getting to the job that funds the mission
20% Personal: Family stuff
65% Mission-Driven: Kyle?s Wish Foundation

Vision alone is cheap. Delivery is everything. TRON proves both must work together.

I got to experience TRON Lightcycle / Run at Disney World today. I had not been to Disney in roughly 20 years. And let me tell you, this was not nostalgia. It was a shock to the system.

If you were a kid in 1982 watching TRON, you remember the feeling. Blue neon. Lightcycles. A digital world that felt impossible.

Back then computers were slow, coding was uncool, and kids who loved tech were often the ones teased. Yet some of us followed that spark anyway.

Fast forward. Now you sit on a Lightcycle and launch into the Grid for real. Smooth acceleration. No clanking lift. A soundtrack that hits at your core. You do not watch TRON here, you're a part of it.

And it works whether you know the films or not. For fans, it is emotional. For newcomers, breathtaking. The staging pulls you out of the real world and into a digital one. The canopy arcs like circuitry. Light and sound move in rhythm. Every detail exists with purpose. 

This attraction only exists because Disney spent 5 years building and refining it. Yeah 5 years. They rerouted a railroad. Engineered new vehicles. Programmed thousands of LEDs. Designed motion, lighting and sound as one system. Tested for safety, for weather, for throughput. Not once, but again and again. It looks effortless because the effort is buried in the experience.

And here is the truth. Almost no company on earth could have done this. Not because others lack imag

If alcohol gets a 21-year-old restriction [in the USA, apply your local restriction] because of its impact on developing brains, then social media deserves the same treatment, maybe more. The numbers are brutal.

Researchers at the University of North Carolina found that heavy social media use repeatedly activates the brain?s dopamine reward pathways in adolescents, shaping their neural wiring the same way early exposure to nicotine and alcohol does.

2. Teen mental health has cratered in lockstep with social media adoption.
CDC Youth Risk Behavior Survey (YRBS) data shows 57% of teen girls report persistent sadness or hopelessness, the highest ever recorded. Teen depression rates have nearly doubled since 2010, the exact period when smartphones and algorithmic feeds exploded.

Correlation isn?t automatically causation, but when two curves overlap this cleanly, it?s not a coincidence.

3. Social media exposure increases depression and self-harm risk.
A landmark 2019 study in JAMA Psychiatry found teens spending over 3 hours a day on social media have a 60% higher risk of developing depression.

1960?s Lava Lamps Protect You on the Internet

In the early 1960s, British inventor and former WWII pilot (you know I love a good aviation connection) Edward Craven Walker spotted something unusual in a Dorset pub. It was a homemade egg timer made from a glass bottle filled with bubbling fluids. While most people ignored it, he saw potential in the strange, unpredictable motion.

He spent years experimenting with waxes, liquids, and light until he perfected what became the Mathmos Astro Lamp, the original lava lamp. His goal wasn?t science or technology. He wanted to capture beauty and movement, to make chaos mesmerizing.

Then something truly magical happened. That same unpredictable motion is now used by Cloudflare to secure the internet.

In their San Francisco headquarters, a wall of more than 100 lamps constantly bubbles and shifts. Cameras record the motion of every glowing blob as it rises, splits, and falls. That unpredictable movement is converted into random data, or digital entropy, which becomes the foundation for cryptographic keys that protect billions of online interactions every day.

According to Mathmos, this process helps encrypt up to 10% of the entire internet.

Here?s why it matters. Computers can?t create true randomness. They only simulate it. Every calculation follows a rule, a seed, a pattern. Without real randomness, encryption can be broken. So Cloudflare turns to the physical world, to chaos itself, for protection.

Walker could never have imagine

And in airports, it actually is. You’re grabbing water, snacks or a charger and racing to a gate. In that context, self-checkout feels almost elegant, speed is the point, expectations are low, and the psychology fits. But big-box retailers made a classic engineering mistake. They assumed a system designed for three-item purchases in a hurry would somehow work for forty items, produce stickers, age checks and real-life stress. They treated shopping like a mechanical workflow instead of a deeply human behavior. Engineering designs for quantity because that math is easy. And that is one important dimension. But quality, especially the quality of a human experience, refuses to live inside a spreadsheet. It’s harder to measure, harder to model, and easier to ignore… until the damage becomes obvious. And that’s exactly what happened. Once your basket crosses 10 items, self-checkout turns into a stress generator. Misreads, PLU codes, weight alerts, frozen screens. The system panics, you panic, and the lone associate overseeing a dozen kiosks becomes a full-time firefighter. And in the process, retailers deleted something irreplaceable: the unscripted human moment. The quick fix. The rescue. The recognition. The small but meaningful interventions that build loyalty. These aren’t “features.” They’re the emotional and cultural infrastructure of a store. And there’s a deeper truth retailers kept ignoring: customers don’t go to stores to be put to work. It was driven by raising the Average Revenue per Job Posting (ARPJ) by approximately 15% . Management calls this "decoupling" of revenue from volume. I call it financial harvesting . The Uncomfortable Truth Honest conversations about what's broken in work, technology, and culture, and how to fix it. AI Didn’t Break Hiring. Legacy ATS Platforms Did. Created on 2025-11-29 14:16 Published on 2025-11-29 14:37 The so-called “AI hiring doom loop,” as reported by Nino Paoli , isn’t caused by candidates, recruiters, or generative AI. It’s driven by something far more basic: companies still don’t know how to define the work, and they’re relying on tools never built for people in the first place. Greenhouse is now sounding the alarm about rising noise, AI-generated applications, and collapsing trust. But let’s be honest. None of this is new. And if you're using their platform I can understand the feeling of "doom." Because these problems were visible a decade ago. High-volume posting, keyword gaming, recruiter overload, vague job descriptions, and broken matching were already there long before ChatGPT wrote its first résumé. Greenhouse saw it in their own customers, in their own funnels, and in their own roadmap gaps. And instead of solving the foundation, they doubled down on ATS mechanics designed for process, not people. So it’s a bit rich to see them warn the industry about an AI-driven crisis when their architecture helped create it. Now they offer tweaks, not transformation, because that’s all a legacy ATS can do. You can’t bolt modern intelligence onto a system built to track steps and expect it to redefine work. AI is here to stay, and we should welcome that. The last thing we need is a return to the hiring practices of 20 years ago: no personalization, slow, biased, inconsistent, and based more on gut than defensible capability. The problem isn’t AI. The problem is the old frameworks we’re forcing it to operate inside. Build the right foundation and AI becomes the most powerful accelerator we’ve ever had. This is why the future isn’t “AI instead of humans.” It’s Applied AI with humans in the lead. Not humans in the loop, not humans as a checkpoin

articles.text: Mission Possible: Break Through Principles to Designing Amazing Candidate Experiences Created on 2021-12-23 01:46 Published on 2021-12-23 13:47 I’m going to be radically honest with you about delivering a great candidate experience. Phenom’s mission is to get a billion people the right job. And we believe we can do that by delivering amazing experiences, for candidates and recruiters alike. When we think of a candidate's first interaction with a company’s talent acquisition function it is likely to be through your digital experience first, and if not, most certainly somewhere along the journey. I want to focus on the technology that helps TA teams define and deliver an engaging candidate experience. When technology was introduced into the talent acquisition domain its primary focus was to replace paper driven processes and record keeping. The first true applicant tracking systems did just that. And that continues today, years later their architectural design is the same. These systems of process and record are still, and in my opinion mistakenly so, the core of the TA tech stack. However, the TA domain has been evolving rapidly over the last decade and we are at the doorstep of a true revolution in the technology itself - more precisely, its purpose and role in TA. So, what is driving this revolution? And how do we know when we have done enough to engage candidates? The driver, simply put, is us as consumers and our expectations in the technologies that deliver experiences. Twenty years ago, not a single delivered platform or point solution in the recruiting space thought candidate experience was important. Systems were designed to replace very tactical recruiter functions. Moving from paper folders to digital filing systems. It took decades to figure out that the intended outcome did not actually help make recruiters more efficient or connect them with quality talent. Enjoy. _______________ I recently made a decision to explore open-market job and project opportunities after several years as a partner in a mid-sized company. My last formal job interview was in 1996, and since then fortune and hard work have paid off. I have been recruited for every job, position or project based solely on my professional reputation and experience, bypassing the cold-candidate interview cycle that so many experienced professionals endure. While I’ve directly hired more than 100 resources in almost twenty years, I myself have not sat in the interviewee seat for a very long time.  And in all that time, nothing has changed!   Many seemingly progressive organizations still have difficulty interviewing experienced job candidates. Why are candidates who would certainly add value to an organization and should be selected instead shown the exit? Or in some cases, why do these same job seekers run for the door after an interview? I wanted to explore one possible reason: The interviewer, and their lack of due diligence. Did you read my resume? I’ve spent the last 25 years, in various capacities and roles, as a technical software design and development professional.
